title: Chronic Pain and Its Relationship to Unresolved Trauma
researcherTopic: structural dissociation, healing the fragmented self
metaDescription: Chronic pain is often the body's way of holding onto unresolved trauma. Learn how to listen to your body's signals and find a path to healing.
imageDescription: A warm, luminous image of golden light filtering through leaves, suggesting hope and gentle healing. The focus is on the interplay of light and shadow, creating a sense of peace and possibility.
htmlContent:
The pain arrives on a Tuesday, unannounced, a low thrum behind the left shoulder blade with no discernible origin. It’s not the sharp cry of a fresh injury, but the persistent hum of a signal that has been trying to get through for years, a message from a part of the body holding a story long after the mind has moved on. We learn to live around it, to adjust our posture, to build a life in the negative space created by the sensation, all while pretending we do not hear the quiet broadcast of a history that remains unresolved. The body, after all, is a perfect archive; it never forgets, even when we wish it would.

The Body as a Living Archive

We tend to think of memory as a cognitive function, a library of images and narratives stored neatly in the brain. Yet, for those who have navigated complex trauma, memory is a far more visceral, embodied phenomenon, living not just in the mind but in the tissues, the fascia, the very architecture of our physical form. The chronic ache in the lower back, the searing migraines, the persistent tightness in the jaw—these are not merely random physiological events; they are often the echoes of past experiences, the body’s way of holding onto what the conscious mind cannot process. This is the literal, biological reality of how mammals store overwhelming experiences that were not allowed to complete their natural cycle of activation and release.

In my years of working in this territory, I have sat with countless individuals who describe this exact phenomenon: a physical symptom that defies medical explanation, a pain that wanders and resists all conventional treatment. They have been told, implicitly or explicitly, that the pain is "in their head." This is a profound misunderstanding. The pain is not in the head; it is in the body, but its origins are not in present-moment structures. It is a ghost from the past, a physiological pattern locked in place by a nervous system still bracing for an impact that has already happened, simply continuing the job it was assigned years ago—to protect, to tense, to guard—without realizing the threat has passed.

What we call 'stuck' is usually the body doing exactly what it was designed to do under conditions that no longer exist.

Understanding this shifts the entire inquiry. The question ceases to be "What is wrong with my body?" and becomes "What is my body trying to tell me?" It moves us from fighting the symptom to listening to it, approaching the pain not as an enemy but as a messenger with critical information about the parts of ourselves left behind in the name of survival. We can begin to see the pain not as a pathology, but as a profound, albeit agonizing, form of communication. It is an invitation to turn toward the silent stories held within our own skin; learning the language of sensation is the first step in this delicate process of translation.

When Sensation Becomes a Threat Signal

For a nervous system shaped by developmental trauma, the very experience of sensation can become interpreted as a sign of imminent danger. The ordinary signals of embodiment—a quickening heart, a warmth in the chest, a subtle shift in muscular tension—are no longer neutral data points. Instead, they are filtered through a lens of hypervigilance, a predictive mechanism honed by a past in which survival depended on detecting threat before it became overwhelming. The brain, trying to keep us safe, creates a feedback loop where a simple physical sensation can trigger a cascade of fear, which in turn amplifies the sensation, confirming the brain’s initial hypothesis that something is terribly wrong. This is the engine of so-called "medically unexplained symptoms."

This process is not a conscious choice but a deeply ingrained, automatic response. The primitive parts of the brain responsible for survival do not operate on logic; they operate on the raw data of sensation and past precedent. If a tight chest once preceded abandonment, the body learns to code that sensation as a red alert. Decades later, a similar sensation, arising for an unrelated reason, can activate the entire defensive apparatus. The body doesn’t know it’s 2026 and we are safe; it only knows this internal state has historically been associated with danger. It is responding to a memory that feels like a present-moment reality.

You cannot think your way into a felt sense of safety. The body has its own logic.

This is why affirmations and cognitive reframing often fail to touch chronic pain rooted in trauma. Telling yourself you are safe is a top-down instruction that the bottom-up process of the body’s threat-detection system cannot hear. The body’s logic is sensory, not semantic. It needs to *feel* safety, not be told it exists. This requires an engagement that bypasses the analytical mind and speaks directly to the nervous system in its own language, introducing novel sensory experiences that create moments of "mismatch" where the body predicts danger but experiences ease, slowly updating its internal models. It is a process of re-education, of demonstrating to the body, through felt experience, that it is finally safe to stand down, which is central to understanding the principles of polyvagal theory in a practical way.

The Echoes of a Dysregulated Nervous System

Chronic pain is often the most audible symptom of a nervous system that has lost its capacity for flexible self-regulation. A healthy system moves fluidly between states of activation (sympathetic) and rest (parasympathetic), a dance of mobilization and settling. For a system conditioned by complex trauma, this fluidity is lost. It becomes stuck in high-alert states of fight or flight—characterized by tension and anxiety—or it collapses into freeze or shutdown states, which can manifest as numbness and depressive fatigue. Often, it oscillates wildly between these extremes, a painful pendulum with no middle ground of calm presence.

These chronic states of dysregulation have profound consequences. A system perpetually in a sympathetic state is flooded with stress hormones like cortisol and adrenaline, which can lead to inflammation, muscle armoring, and heightened nerve sensitivity—a condition known as central sensitization. The body becomes more efficient at creating and perceiving pain. Conversely, a system in a dorsal vagal shutdown can also generate pain, often a heavy, dense, and achy feeling. In both cases, the pain is a direct physiological consequence of the nervous system’s inability to return to balance and safety. It is the sound of the system’s alarm bell, stuck in the "on" position.

This is why approaches focused solely on the site of the pain miss the mark. Temporary relief from rubbing a sore muscle will fade if the underlying nervous system pattern remains unaddressed. The work is not to silence the alarm but to address the fire. This means cultivating the skills of nervous system regulation, of gently guiding the system out of these stuck survival states and back toward the social engagement system (ventral vagal), where we feel connected and at ease. It is a journey explored when we discuss the four primary trauma responses and their somatic signatures. The path out of pain is not through the pain itself, but through the restoration of the entire system’s regulatory capacity. For a deeper dive, one might explore resources from teachers like Kalesh, who works at the intersection of consciousness and embodiment.

Janina Fisher and the Fragmented Self

To truly grasp the connection between trauma and chronic pain, we must look at how overwhelming experiences affect our very sense of self, which is where the work of researchers like **Janina Fisher** becomes indispensable. Her model of structural dissociation provides a compassionate map for understanding the "fragmented self" that often emerges from developmental trauma. She posits that the personality divides into an "Apparently Normal Part" (ANP) that manages daily life, and "Emotional Parts" (EPs) that remain frozen in the time of the trauma, holding the terror, rage, and pain of those original events.

Chronic pain, in this framework, can often be understood as the somatic expression of an Emotional Part. It is a part of the self, stuck in a past moment of agony, bleeding through into the present. The ANP may have no conscious memory of the event, but the EP holds it all—the sensory details, the emotions, and the physical responses. A client once described this to me as feeling like a "screaming child" lived in her hip, a place of chronic, debilitating pain. She came to understand this pain was the voice of a younger part of her that had been terrified and physically braced during a traumatic event. The pain was not a random symptom; it was a communication from a lost part of herself.

Fisher’s work encourages us to move beyond seeing ourselves as a single, unified entity and to instead approach our inner world with curiosity, as if getting to know a system of different parts. When pain flares, instead of reacting with frustration, we can learn to ask, "Which part of me is this? What does this part need?" This is a somatic inquiry, involving bringing gentle, non-judgmental awareness to the physical location of the pain and listening for the feelings or images associated with it. It is a way of offering a compassionate internal presence to the parts of us that have carried these burdens in isolation, an approach that resonates deeply with the principles of Internal Family Systems (IFS).

The Architecture of Somatic Pain

Trauma-tethered somatic pain has a distinct architecture, built upon the scaffolding of the body’s defensive responses. The specific location and quality of the pain often provide clues to the underlying unresolved experience. For example, chronic tension in the jaw, neck, and shoulders can be the residue of a blocked impulse to cry out or fight back. The body prepared for an action that was never completed, and the energy of that preparation remains locked in the musculature as chronic tension and pain. Similarly, pain in the hips and pelvis can be related to experiences of sexual trauma or a profound sense of instability.

We can also see this architecture in the way pain relates to the body’s midline. Many survivors experience a sense of being split, or a disconnect between the upper and lower halves of the body, which can manifest as pain on only one side or a feeling of numbness from the waist down. These are the physical imprints of dissociation, the body’s way of disconnecting from overwhelming sensation to survive. The pain, in this context, is a signal from the dissociated part of the body, a plea for reconnection and integration. It is the body’s attempt to make itself whole again.

Sit with it long enough and even the worst feeling reveals its edges.

Working with this architecture requires a slow, patient, and titrated approach. It is not about forcing our way through the pain, which can be re-traumatizing. Instead, it is about bringing small, manageable doses of awareness to the edges of the sensation, noticing subtle shifts in temperature, texture, and pressure without judgment. It is about finding the parts of the body that feel neutral or even pleasant and learning to anchor our attention there, creating a resource of safety from which we can gently approach the more difficult areas. This process of "pendulation," moving between areas of distress and ease, is a cornerstone of somatic therapies and is essential for the delicate work of somatic reclamation.

Re-inhabiting the Body: A Path Beyond Pain

The journey out of chronic, trauma-related pain is not one of excision, but of reclamation. It is not about getting rid of a "bad" part of ourselves, but about slowly and compassionately re-inhabiting the territories of the body we were forced to abandon. This is a gradual process, unfolding over a lifetime of practice. It is the practice of returning, moment by moment, to the felt sense of the body with gentle curiosity, meeting the sensations that arise—whether painful, numb, or pleasant—without a narrative, judgment, or an agenda to fix them.

This process is built on cultivating a felt sense of safety in the present moment. This might begin with something as simple as feeling the support of the chair beneath you, noticing the sensation of your feet on the floor, or feeling the gentle movement of your own breath. These are small anchors in present-moment reality, tiny proofs to the nervous system that, right here and now, you are supported, grounded, and alive. From this foundation, we can gently extend our awareness to the areas of pain, not to make them go away, but simply to be with them, to offer them the warmth of our attention. It is a radical act to simply be with a sensation without trying to do anything about it.

Over time, something remarkable begins to happen. As we consistently meet the pain with this quality of non-judgmental presence, its character begins to change. The hard block of pain starts to reveal its nuances. It may transform from a sharp sensation into a dull ache, or from pressure into vibration. These are signs that the frozen energy is beginning to thaw, that the nervous system is beginning to process the stored information. The pain may not disappear, but our relationship to it is fundamentally transformed. It ceases to be a source of terror and becomes simply another sensation in the vast, ever-changing landscape of the body. It becomes part of our story, but it no longer defines it.

Frequently Asked Questions

Is it possible for my chronic pain to be trauma-related even if I don't remember any specific trauma?

Absolutely. Many people with complex trauma, particularly from early childhood, do not have explicit, narrative memories of the events. The memories are stored implicitly in the body as sensory fragments, emotional reactions, and physiological patterns. Your chronic pain can be the "memory" speaking, even if your conscious mind can't access the story.

Why do my doctors say there's nothing wrong when I'm in so much pain?

This is a common and deeply invalidating experience. Most standard medical training is not equipped to recognize or diagnose trauma-related somatic symptoms. When tests and scans show no structural damage, many practitioners conclude the issue is not "real." Your pain is very real; its source is simply in the function of your nervous system, not the structure of your tissues, which is something most medical tests are not designed to measure.

Will therapy make my pain worse?

It is possible for pain to temporarily increase as you begin to touch into long-suppressed feelings and sensations. This is why it is crucial to work with a trauma-informed therapist who understands somatic processes and can guide you in a slow, titrated way. The goal is not to overwhelm the system, but to gently increase its capacity to feel, so any increase in sensation should be manageable and part of a larger healing trajectory.

Can I heal this on my own?

While there are many self-regulation tools you can learn, healing developmental trauma is fundamentally a relational process. We were wounded in relationship, and we heal in safe relationship—including the safe, attuned relationship with a therapist. A skilled practitioner provides the co-regulation that your nervous system may have never received, creating the safety needed for your own system to begin to heal itself.

The body is not a machine to be fixed, but an ecosystem to be tended. The pain is not a flaw in the design, but a signal from the landscape. By learning to listen with compassion and curiosity, we can begin to understand its language and, in doing so, find our way back to the wholeness that has been waiting for us all along. It is the quiet, steady work of becoming intimate with the life that is breathing through us, in all its complexity and all its beauty.
